text.title: Kernel Tuning (sysctl) for VPS Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules every application of `Kernel Tuning (sysctl) for VPS` must enforce. Each rule pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Tuning MUST live in `/etc/sysctl.d/60-vps-tuning.conf`, not `/etc/sysctl.conf`. Edits to main conf = REJECT.
rule.rationale: Main /etc/sysctl.conf is for distro defaults; drop-ins survive distro upgrades cleanly.

rule.statement: Every non-comment line MUST be preceded by a comment naming the symptom it fixes. Bare numbers = REJECT.
rule.rationale: A bare `kernel.pid_max = 4194304` six months later is mysterious; rationale makes it audit-ready.

rule.statement: If enabling BBR (`net.ipv4.tcp_congestion_control = bbr`), also set `net.core.default_qdisc = fq`. Missing fq = REJECT.
rule.rationale: BBR without fq qdisc misbehaves under packet bursts; pairing is mandatory.

rule.statement: After `sysctl --system`, each key MUST be verified with `sysctl <key>`. Apply-without-verify = REJECT.
rule.rationale: Invalid syntax silently ignored on some keys; verify confirms intended state.

rule.statement: Edits MUST be reproducible from the drop-in file in source control. Ad-hoc `sysctl -w` = REJECT.
rule.rationale: Ephemeral `sysctl -w` evaporates on reboot; file in /etc/sysctl.d/ + repo copy is durable + reviewable.

rule.statement: When preconditions in `Applies If` fail OR any item in `Skip If` triggers, do not run this methodology. Document the skip rationale in the artefact's parent log.
rule.rationale: Misapplied methodologies are worse than no methodology — they consume cycles and produce false confidence.
